title: Qualitative vs Quantitative Research
definition: Qual explores why and how (interviews); quant measures how many and how often (surveys, analytics).
example: Interviews reveal why users abandon checkout (qual); a funnel dashboard shows 40% drop at payment step (quant) — you need both to prioritize fixes.
whenToUse: Qual for discovery; quant for validation and sizing.
interviewPrompts: When would you use a survey vs an interview?

title: Discovery Interview Techniques
definition: Open-ended, past-behavior-focused conversations to uncover unmet needs and workarounds.
example: Instead of 'Do you like dashboards?', you ask 'Walk me through the last time you prepared a weekly metrics report for your manager.'
whenToUse: Early discovery when you need to understand problems deeply.
interviewPrompts: Tell me about the last time you...
officeUse: User interviews, customer calls, win/loss debriefs.

title: The Mom Test
definition: Ask about past behavior and specifics, not opinions or hypotheticals — so users can't lie to please you.
example: Bad: 'Would you pay for this?' Good: 'What did you spend on tools for this problem last year?' — concrete past behavior beats polite encouragement.
whenToUse: All discovery interviews; especially early problem validation.
frameworkSteps: Talk about their life, not your idea
interviewPrompts: Tell me about the last time you...
officeUse: Interview guide design; coaching teams on customer conversations.

title: Research Synthesis
definition: Turning raw interview notes into themes, insights, and opportunities.
example: After 5 interviews, you cluster notes into 'approval bottlenecks' and 'lack of visibility' — writing insight statements that feed your opportunity solution tree.
officeUse: Research readouts and OST opportunity mapping.
